narrative: I'm sorry to deliver this news, but your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 54% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is just over the threshold we use to approve admissions. This prediction is based on your profile: you're a male White student from an upper-middle-income family, planning to attend full-time, with an undergraduate GPA of 3.50—which is actually stronger than the typical applicant's 3.21—an LSAT score of 35.50 slightly below the average 36.70, and notably lower rankings in your school's grade deciles for Year 1 (3rd decile versus the average 6th) and Year 3 (2nd decile versus the average 6th). Compared to typical students in our dataset, your law school performance deciles stand out as much weaker, pulling you below the profiles of those predicted to pass, while your solid undergrad record and demographic factors align more closely with the norm. The model's decision was most heavily influenced by your Year 3 decile rank, which had the strongest push toward predicting bar failure, followed very closely by your Year 1 decile rank doing the same. Your LSAT score came next in influence, also nudging the prediction toward failure, with family income bracket, undergraduate GPA, racial background, gender, and full-time status each contributing progressively less but still in the direction of higher failure risk. In essence, no single feature strongly favored passage; instead, the combination, led by your lower law school deciles, tipped the scales. This ranking shows how your early and later law school grades were the dominant signals the model weighed most heavily against you. To improve your future chances of passing the bar, focus on boosting your law school performance—perhaps by seeking tutoring, better study habits, or time management strategies to climb those decile rankings in upcoming years. Retaking the LSAT for a higher score could also help offset this in a future application, as it was a close third in influence. Your strong undergrad GPA is a great foundation, so building on that with dedicated bar prep courses or practice exams might realistically shift predictions in your favor next time. Hang in there; many students turn things around with targeted effort.
